One Click. $4.88 Million.

Cybercrime is now the world's third-largest economy. This course gives you the knowledge to stop being its next data point.

The $10.5 Trillion Problem

$10.5T

Projected cybercrime costs in 2025

$4.88M

Average cost of a single data breach

1,968

Cyberattacks per organization per week

Bigger Than Japan's GDP

If cybercrime were a country, it would rank third globally — behind only the United States and China. At $10.5 trillion projected for 2025, the cybercrime economy has grown faster than most legitimate industries.

This isn't a technology problem. It's a business survival problem. And no organization is too small to be a target: 43% of all cyberattacks hit small businesses, and 60% of those businesses never recover.

Anatomy of a $3 Billion Breach

01 / 04

The Entry Point

February 2024. Attackers from the ALPHV/BlackCat group accessed Change Healthcare — a company processing half of all U.S. medical claims — through compromised remote access credentials. Multi-factor authentication wasn't enabled on the targeted application.

02 / 04

The Blast Radius

Claims processing stopped for 900,000 physicians, 33,000 pharmacies, and 5,500 hospitals. 80% of physician practices lost revenue from unpaid claims. 94% of healthcare providers reported direct financial impact.

03 / 04

The Double Extortion

UnitedHealth Group paid a $22 million ransom. It didn't matter. The attackers' affiliate retained the stolen data and re-extorted through a different group called RansomHub. 190 million patient records were exposed — later revised to 259 million.

04 / 04

The True Cost

Total cost: $3.09 billion. The ransom payment was the smallest line item. Initial estimates of $1.6 billion proved wildly optimistic as the true scope kept expanding over months. One missing MFA checkbox triggered the entire cascade.

“
A single stolen credential cost UnitedHealth $3.09 billion and exposed 190 million patient records. The ransom payment was 0.7% of the total damage.

— Change Healthcare Breach Analysis

How They Get In

90% of Breaches Start Here

Not with a zero-day exploit. Not with advanced malware. With a human being making a split-second decision to click, call, or trust.

The Attack Playbook

Phishing

Over 90% of successful cyberattacks begin with a phishing email. In 2025, AI-generated phishing emails achieve a 54% click-through rate versus just 12% for human-written messages. AI has eliminated the grammar errors and formatting mistakes that were once reliable red flags.

1.13 million phishing attacks were recorded in Q2 2025 alone — the highest quarterly total since tracking began. The average phishing-related breach costs $4.8 million.

Spear Phishing

Spear phishing represents only 0.1% of phishing volume but causes 66% of all breaches. These targeted attacks use personal details — your job title, current projects, your manager's name — to craft messages that feel genuinely personal.

Volume-based spam filters catch mass phishing effectively. They're nearly useless against the attacks that actually succeed. The 25% surge in spear phishing in 2024 reflects attackers investing in quality over quantity.

Vishing

Voice phishing attacks exploded 442% between the first and second half of 2024. Phone calls bypass email security entirely and exploit the natural human tendency to be helpful.

The MGM Resorts breach began with exactly this: attackers found an employee on LinkedIn, called the IT help desk pretending to be that person, and asked for a password reset. Cost: $100 million.

Social Engineering

Social engineering was the #1 initial access vector in 2024-2025, responsible for 36% of all cyber intrusions according to Palo Alto's Unit 42. It surpassed both malware and software vulnerability exploitation.

The playbook exploits three cognitive shortcuts: authority bias (fake executive emails), urgency ('respond by noon'), and familiarity (hijacked vendor accounts). These operate below conscious awareness.

The $100 Million Phone Call

In September 2023, the Scattered Spider group didn't use zero-day exploits or advanced malware. They found an MGM employee on LinkedIn, called the IT help desk pretending to be that person, and asked for a password reset.

Within hours, they controlled critical systems. Slot machines, ATMs, room keys, and payment systems across Las Vegas properties failed for nearly a week. Rival Caesars paid a $15 million ransom from a similar attack the same month.

MGM later committed $50 million to cybersecurity upgrades — half the cost of the breach itself.

Who Actually Falls for Phishing?

The assumptions are wrong. Digital natives are more vulnerable than their parents.

Common AssumptionReality
Older employees are the primary targetsWidely believedGen-Z has the highest victim rate at 23%
Technical users are immuneAssumed safeOverconfidence creates faster, less cautious clicking

Bad grammar gives phishing awayHistorical red flagAI eliminated this — 82.6% of phishing uses AI now

The Ransomware Economy

Crime as a Service

Ransomware is no longer the work of lone hackers. It's an industrialized criminal economy with franchise models, customer support desks, and quarterly revenue targets.

Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) platforms let criminal groups rent pre-built ransomware tools to affiliates who execute attacks. When law enforcement disrupted LockBit in early 2024 — reducing its payments by 79% — RansomHub simply absorbed former LockBit and BlackCat affiliates to become the most active group. The ecosystem is hydra-headed: cut one and others grow.

How a Modern Ransomware Attack Works

01
Initial Access
Compromised credentials, phishing, or third-party vendor exploit. 41.4% of attacks now start through supply chain partners.
arrow_downward
02
Lateral Movement
Attackers move through the network for days or weeks, mapping systems, identifying backup locations, and escalating privileges.
arrow_downward
03
Data Exfiltration
Before encrypting anything, attackers steal sensitive data. This enables the second extortion lever — threatening public release.
arrow_downward
04
Backup Destruction
98% of ransomware cases involve attempts to corrupt or delete backups. This is the step that determines whether the victim pays.
arrow_downward
05
Encryption + Demand
Systems are locked. A ransom note appears. But the encryption rate is falling — from 70% to 50% — as pure data theft extortion doubles.

Three Attacks That Changed Everything

Colonial Pipeline (2021): When Fuel Stopped Flowing expand_more

On May 7, 2021, the DarkSide group accessed Colonial Pipeline — which delivers 45% of East Coast fuel — through a compromised VPN password without MFA. The company paid $4.4 million in Bitcoin within hours.

Gas prices hit a 6-year high of $3.04/gallon. 10,600 stations ran dry across 17 states. Emergency declarations were issued. The DOJ later recovered 64 of 75 bitcoins by tracing transactions — proving cryptocurrency isn't as anonymous as criminals assumed.

The lesson: A single missing MFA checkbox on an inactive VPN account caused a national infrastructure crisis.

MOVEit (2023): The Supply Chain Bomb expand_more

The CL0P gang exploited a zero-day vulnerability in MOVEit file transfer software — a tool used by thousands of organizations. Over 2,700 organizations were compromised and 93.3 million individuals had data exposed. Estimated total cost: $12.15 billion.

CL0P abandoned encryption entirely and relied solely on data theft extortion, hitting the BBC, British Airways, and multiple U.S. government agencies through a single software vulnerability.

The lesson: Your security is only as strong as your least-secure vendor. Supply chain risk isn't theoretical — it's the dominant attack surface.

Change Healthcare (2024): The Breach That Kept Getting Worse expand_more

BlackCat/ALPHV encrypted Change Healthcare, exposing data of 190 million Americans (later revised to 259 million). UnitedHealth reported $2.457 billion in costs. Despite paying a $22 million ransom, the attackers' affiliate retained data and re-extorted through RansomHub.

94% of healthcare providers reported financial impact. Initial cost estimates kept doubling as the scope expanded over months.

The lesson: Paying a ransom guarantees nothing when affiliate-based RaaS models mean multiple parties hold your data.

35%

Drop in ransomware payments (victims refusing to pay)

50%

Increase in U.S. ransomware attacks (more attempts, less profit each)

63%

Organizations that refused to pay ransoms in 2024

Building the Castle

Defense in Depth: Not One Wall, But Five

A castle's security never relied on a single wall. It layered a moat, drawbridge with guards, inner walls, archers on towers, and a fortified keep for treasure.

Modern cybersecurity works identically. Firewalls alone fail. But firewalls + MFA + endpoint detection + encrypted backups + trained staff create compounding protection. Healthcare entities that adopted multi-layered strategies in 2024 reported a 93% decrease in data breaches compared to single-layer protections.

NIST CSF 2.0: The Six Functions

bolt

Govern

The new addition. Establishes cybersecurity risk management as an organizational priority with leadership accountability, supply chain oversight, and measurable policies.

speed

Identify

Know your assets, data flows, and vulnerabilities. You can't protect what you don't know exists. Asset inventory and risk assessment are the foundation.

shield

Protect

Deploy safeguards: access controls, encryption, training, secure configurations. Defense in depth lives here — multiple overlapping controls across physical, technical, and administrative layers.

memory

Detect

Monitor for anomalies, events, and potential incidents continuously. The faster you detect, the smaller the blast radius. Continuous monitoring is the Zero Trust mandate.

hub

Respond

Execute your incident response plan. Contain, analyze, communicate, and mitigate. NIST SP 800-61 Rev 3 (April 2025) maps response to the full CSF lifecycle.

star

Recover

Restore capabilities and services. The 3-2-1-1-0 backup rule — 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite, 1 immutable, 0 errors verified — is the last line of defense against ransomware.

The Language of Defense

Zero Trust

A security architecture that eliminates implicit trust by continuously validating every access request. Principle: 'Never trust, always verify.' 63% of organizations have adopted it at least partially by 2025.

CIA Triad

The three goals every security control maps to: Confidentiality (only authorized access), Integrity (data is accurate and unaltered), and Availability (systems accessible when needed). Ransomware attacks all three simultaneously.

Least Privilege

Granting users and systems only the minimum access needed for their specific role. Reduces lateral movement — the primary way ransomware spreads from one compromised endpoint to entire networks.

Network Segmentation

Dividing a network into isolated zones so a breach in one segment cannot spread to others. A major bank used this to cut PCI audit prep time by 40%.

Zero Trust Architecture

1
User Identity
2
Devices
3

4

6

Automation

The Password Is Dead. Long Live the Passkey.

1 in 5 Login Attempts Is an Attack

Analysis of SSO provider logs reveals that 19% of all authentication attempts are credential stuffing — automated bots trying stolen username/password pairs. Passwords aren't just weak. They're under industrial-scale assault every second of every day.

The Authentication Hierarchy

01
Passwords Alone

The weakest link. 22% of confirmed breaches in 2025 used compromised credentials. Infostealer malware stole 1.8 billion credentials this year alone — an 800% surge. Password-only authentication is an open door.

02
SMS-Based MFA

Better than nothing, but interceptable via SIM swap attacks. It's the minimum tier, not the target. Attackers can port your phone number in minutes.

03
Authenticator App (TOTP)

Significantly stronger. Time-based one-time passwords aren't interceptable via SIM swap. But they're still phishable — real-time proxy attacks can capture and replay codes within their 30-second window.

04
Push Notifications

Convenient, but vulnerable to MFA fatigue bombing — where attackers flood victims with push requests until they approve one out of frustration. This appears in 14% of security incidents in the 2025 Verizon DBIR.

05
Hardware Security Keys

Phishing-resistant. The key won't respond to a phishing site because it validates the domain cryptographically. The downside: physical keys can be lost, and organizations must manage backup methods.

06
Passkeys (FIDO2/WebAuthn)

The destination. Phishing-resistant, synced across devices, and 3x faster than passwords. Google reports 800 million accounts using passkeys. Microsoft says passkey login is 8x faster than password-plus-MFA. 87% of businesses have deployed or are deploying them.

Test Your Knowledge: Authentication

info
What percentage of login attempts are actually credential stuffing attacks?
Tap to reveal

19% — nearly one in five authentication attempts on major platforms is automated credential stuffing.

local_fire_department
Why is SMS-based MFA considered the weakest form of multi-factor authentication?
Tap to reveal

SMS codes can be intercepted via SIM swap attacks, where an attacker convinces your carrier to port your number to their device.

pan_tool
What is MFA fatigue bombing?
Tap to reveal

Flooding a victim with push notification MFA requests until they approve one out of frustration. It appears in 14% of security incidents (2025 Verizon DBIR).

bolt
How many credentials did infostealer malware steal in 2025?
Tap to reveal

1.8 billion credentials from 5.8 million devices — an 800% surge. Stolen passwords and session cookies now figure in 86% of breaches.

shield
What makes passkeys phishing-resistant?
Tap to reveal

Passkeys use public-key cryptography bound to the legitimate domain. They cryptographically verify the site, so a phishing page can never trigger the authentication — the key simply won't respond.

Your Data Is a Liability

Everything You Don't Collect Can't Be Stolen

Data minimization is like packing for a trip: everything you don't bring can't get lost. Organizations collect data like over-packers — 'just in case we need it.' But every piece of unnecessary data is a liability in a breach, a compliance burden under GDPR/CCPA, and a storage cost.

82% of organizations report that data minimization reduces their regulatory risk exposure. Yet only 26% use privacy-by-design principles in new product development, despite it being a GDPR requirement since 2018.

The Human Firewall

3%

Improvement from training alone (Microsoft)

86%

Improvement from training + culture change (KnowBe4)

$19.5M

Average annual cost of insider threats per organization

From 32% to 5%: The Training Curve That Works

The average phish-prone percentage starts at 32.4%. After initial training, it drops to 17.6%. After a full year of monthly training combined with weekly simulated phishing tests, it reaches 5% — an 86% total reduction.

The secret isn't the training content. It's the frequency, the practice, and the organizational culture that makes reporting phishing a celebrated behavior rather than an embarrassing admission. Organizations with effective programs are 8.3x less likely to appear on public breach lists.

Checkbox Training vs. Security Culture

Checkbox ComplianceSecurity Culture
Phishing click reduction3%86%
FrequencyAnnual moduleMonthly training + weekly simulations
Reporting cultureFear of blameCelebrated behavior
Leadership involvementcancelcheck_circle
Measurable behavior changecancelcheck_circle
Breach list appearanceNo measurable impact8.3x less likely

The Insider Threat You're Not Watching For

The Numbers: $19.5 Million Per Year Per Organization expand_more

The 2025 Ponemon study recorded 7,868 insider incidents — double the 3,269 in 2018. Organizations average 14.5 insider-related incidents per year. The annual cost of $19.5 million per organization includes investigation, containment, remediation, and business disruption.

75% Aren't Malicious — They're Negligent expand_more

Non-malicious insiders cause three-quarters of incidents: negligent employees (55%) who make mistakes, and externally exploited employees (20%) who are manipulated through social engineering. Only 25% are intentionally malicious. This means the biggest insider risk responds to training and culture change, not just surveillance.

Speed Is Worth $8.1 Million expand_more

The average insider incident takes 81 days to resolve. Incidents contained in under 31 days cost $10.6 million versus $18.7 million for those lasting over 91 days. That $8.1 million gap is the ROI for cross-functional insider threat teams combining security, HR, legal, and management. Every day of delay costs roughly $90,000.

The Dark Web Is Recruiting Your Employees expand_more

Flashpoint observed 91,321 instances of insider recruiting, advertising, and threat actor discussions on the dark web in 2025. Shadow AI and unmanaged AI agents are amplifying insider risk as AI adoption outpaces visibility and governance.

Your Move

The Difference Between a $10,000 Incident and a $10 Million Breach

It's not the attack. It's the preparation. Immutable backups. Tested recovery plans. Network segmentation. Phishing-resistant authentication. And a culture where reporting suspicious activity is rewarded, not punished.
